coredata.dc:title: Impedimetric aptasensor for HER2 biomarker using graphene quantum dots, polypyrrole and cobalt phthalocyanine modified electrodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Centane, Sixolile
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: A method is presented for the electrochemical detection of the breast cancer biomarker human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2). A glassy carbon electrode was modified using two techniques known as sequential adsorption and electro-polymerization, and the results are compared. The highly conductive polypyrrole (PPy) is used, in the presence of sulfur/nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs) and a known cobalt phthalocyanine (CoPc). The different nanomaterials were used as an immobilization platform for the HER2 specific HB5 aptamer via amide linkage. The nanomaterials were arranged in various ways on the glassy carbon electrode, to investigate the effect of the electrode interface on the operational characteristics of a biosensor. The immobilized aptamer selectively recognizes HER2 on the electrode interface, and this leads to an increased charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the electrode when using ferricyanide as the electrochemical probe. The developed immunosensors showed high sensitivity with the best detection limit of 0.00141 ng/mL. The results showed that the method is simple and sensitive enough for the determination of HER2 in serum samples with good reproducibility and accuracy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptasensor
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
Co phthalocyanine
Human epidermal growth factor receptor 2
Human serum
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrodes modified by polymerization of pyrrole in the presence of SNGQDs or CoPc, followed by adsorption
Aptamer immobilization on to the modified glassy carbon electrodes

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Republic of South Africa

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology ( UID 62620 ), and Rhodes University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CV diagrams for the generation of (A) GCE/PPy in 0.15 M pyrrole and (B) GCE/PPy@CoPc in 0.15 M pyrrole and 1 μg/mL CoPc (in DMSO). Both studies were done in PBS pH 7.4, 10 cycles at 100 mV/s.

(A) Cyclic voltammogram and (B) Nyquist pots of the various modified electrodes: GCE (a), GCE/PPy (1) (b), GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4) (c), GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc (6) (d), GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4)/HB5 (e), and GCE/PPy@SNGQDs(4)/HB5/HER2, [HER2] = 1 ng/mL (f). All in 1 mM Fe(CN)6]3−/[Fe(CN)6]4− in 0.1 mol·L−1 KCl in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4).

Equivalent circuit adopted to fit the impedance data where Rs is the ohmic resistance of the electrolyte solution, Q the phase constant element, W the Warburg impedance, and Rct the electron-transfer resistance. Showing fitted data for the GCE/PPy/SNGQDs 8 (seq) as an example.

Nyquist plots of HER2 concentrations for (a) GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq) (9)/HB5 and (b). GCE/PPy@CoPc/SNGQDs (7)/HB5 and the corresponding calibration plots of log HER2 vs. Rct using selected concentrations. All in Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (1 mM in 0.1 mol·L−1 KCl in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4).
Graphic representation of the GCE modification by PPy/nanomaterial copolymerization for the fabrication of the GCE/PPy@SNGQDs and GCE/PPy@CoPc probes.
Steps towards the fabrication of the GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc/HB5 apt/HER2.
Peak potential separations and Rct values in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−].

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Brackets represent Rct values of the corresponding probes with aptamer (concentration 1 μM) prior HER2 detection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Limit of detection (LOD) and sensitivities for detection of HER2 biosensors developed in this work. Linear range 1–10 ng/mL in all cases.
Main features between existing HER2 biosensors and biosensor developed in this work.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: DPV = differential pulse voltammetry, SWV = square wave voltammetry.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: LR = linear range, LOD = limit of detection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Percentage recoveries for the detection of HER2 in spiked human serum.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SDEV = standard deviation. RSD = relative standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Impedimetric aptasensor for HER2 biomarker using graphene quantum dots, polypyrrole and cobalt phthalocyanine modified electrodes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Chemistry Department, Rhodes University, Grahamstown 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Chemistry Department, Rhodes University, Grahamstown, 6140 South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A method is presented for the electrochemical detection of the breast cancer biomarker human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2). A glassy carbon electrode was modified using two techniques known as sequential adsorption and electro-polymerization, and the results are compared. The highly conductive polypyrrole (PPy) is used, in the presence of sulfur/nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs) and a known cobalt phthalocyanine (CoPc). The different nanomaterials were used as an immobilization platform for the HER2 specific HB5 aptamer via amide linkage. The nanomaterials were arranged in various ways on the glassy carbon electrode, to investigate the effect of the electrode interface on the operational characteristics of a biosensor. The immobilized aptamer selectively recognizes HER2 on the electrode interface, and this leads to an increased charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the electrode when using ferricyanide as the electrochemical probe. The developed immunosensors showed high sensitivity with the best detection limit of 0.00141 ng/mL. The results showed that the method is simple and sensitive enough for the determination of HER2 in serum samples with good reproducibility and accuracy.

ce:section.ce:para: The application of biosensors for early detection of breast cancer is a continuously growing research through the development of new detection techniques. Electrochemical biosensors in particular are superior compared to other biosensors owing to their simplicity, inexpensiveness, robustness, and small sample volume [1–3]. In this work we design a sensor for an established breast cancer biomarker HER2 (human epidermal growth factor receptor 2). The HER2 biomarker is over expressed in 20–30% of breast cancers, and has been classified as both a prognostic and predictive biomarker in breast cancer [4,5]. An HB5 DNA aptamer (defined below), which is HER2 specific has been chosen as biorecognition element of interest. Aptamers as biorecognition elements possess high affinity and selectivity towards their targets [6]. They often undergo significant conformational changes upon target binding. This offers great flexibility in design of novel biosensors with high detection sensitivity and selectivity. In addition, aptamers are cheaper and easier to synthesize compared to antibodies [7]. The sensor materials in the current study are graphene quantum dots, a cobalt based phthalocyanine and polypyrrole. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess chemical groups on their edge that act as sites for functionalization [8], and they enhance electron transfer. It has also been reported that doping of pristine graphene quantum dots with heteroatoms such as nitrogen [9] and sulfur [10] can further enhance electron transfer, hence SNGQDs are employed in this work. GQDs enhance the activity of other electrocatalysts such as metallophthalocyanines (MPcs) [11]. MPcs are well known electrocatalysts, especially those containing electroactive central metal such as Co employed in this work [12]. In addition, the functional groups on the phthalocyanine ring were tailored specifically, to maximize interaction between the phthalocyanine and the biorecognition element. The carboxylic group on the phthalocyanine allow for covalent linkage to the aptamer via an amide linkage. Similarly, GQDs have carboxylic groups on their edges, and an amide linkage will be formed with the aptamer as well. Polypyrrole (PPy) is a well-known conductive polymer [13,14]. It possesses excellent stimulus responsive properties which makes it a very smart biomaterial. In addition, the polymer has good in vitro and in vivo biocompatibility [15–17]. A number of PPy composites have been prepared and their mechanical, electrical, or electrochemical properties investigated [18–20]. Electropolymerized pyrrole has been reported to improve electrocatalytic activity and stability of the electrode surface [21]. In this work, the electropolymerization of pyrrole in the presence of SNGQDs, as well as in the presence of a cobalt phthalocyanine (CoPc) is investigated towards the electrochemical detection of HER2 on a glassy carbon electrode. GQDs based nanocarrier labelled with Herceptin and β-cyclodextrin has been employed for the detection of HER2 [22]. This work reports for the first time on the use of PPy, CoPc and SNGQDs for the detection of HER2. The role of the CoPc (the structure shown in Scheme 1 ) in this work is to enhance electron transfer. Pc molecules are known to have good electronic properties [12]. CoPc derivatives in particular have superiour electrocatalytic activity [12]. MPc derivatives have been linked to an aptamer for human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) detection [23]. The combination of metallophthalocyanines with various nanoparticles that possess electrocatalytic properties have been shown two improve the performance of the electrochemical sensors [24]. And as such, this work takes advantage of the synergetic combination of the properties of phthalocyanines, polypyrrole and SNGQDs as electrode modifiers for the electrochemical biosensing of HER2.

The aim of this work is to investigate the contribution of sensor interface on the operational characteristics of the glassy carbon electrode based HER2 sensor. The performance of GCE based sensors where: (i) GCE is modified via polymerization of pyrrole in the presence of SNGQDs (represented as PPy@SNGQDs) or CoPc (represented as PPy@CoPc), and (ii) GCE is modified via sequential adsorption of nanomaterials following pyrrole electropolymerization is investigated and compared. A complete list of electrodes is presented in Table 1 .

The following materials were purchased Sigma Aldrich: Pyrrole, human serum, bovine serum albumin (BSA), N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, NaCl, KCl (K4[Fe(CN)6]), (K3[Fe(CN)6]), the protein (human epidermal growth factor receptor 2, HER2). The HB5 DNA aptamer (5′−/5 AmMC6/AAC CGC CCA AAT CCC TAA GAG TCT GCA CTT GTC ATT TTG TAT ATG TAT TTG GTT TTT GGC TCT CAC AGA CAC ACT ACA CAC GCA CA-3′) was purchased from Integrated DNA technologies (IDT), South Africa. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4) was prepared using weighed amounts of Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, KCl and NaCl. Millipore water was obtained from Type II water which was obtained from an Elga PURELAB Chorus 2 (RO/DI) system. The syntheses of SNGQDs and the cobalt tetra carboxyphenoxy phthalocyanine have been reported [25,26]. The HB5 DNA aptamer was prepared according to the manufacturer's instructions for 100 μM in 10 mM PBS of pH 7.4. HER2 protein (specific target) was sold as a solution, with concentration of 4.4 mg/mL: necessary dilutions were made using PBS (pH 7.4). The 1% BSA for non-specific binding was made using PBS solution (pH 7.4). All stock solutions were made using Millipore water and were kept at 20 °C during the course of the study.

Ground state electronic absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Discover equipped with a Lynx-Eye Detector, using CuKa radiation (= 1.5405 Å, nickel filter). The morphologies of nanoparticles were determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a Zeiss Libra 120 TEM operating at 80 kV. The immunosensor and modified electrodes were characterized using electrochemical methods. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic Voltammetry (CV) studies were performed using an Autolab Potentiostat PGSTAT30 equipped with Nova software version.

A three electrode electrochemical cell consisting of Ag|AgCl (3 M KCl) as reference electrode, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (geometric area of 0.071 cm2) as the working electrode and a platinum wire as the counter electrode was used. Representations of the fabrication of the sensors are shown in Schemes 1 and 2 . A glassy carbon electrode was first polished with Al2O3 powder (Aldrich, 0.1, 0.05 mm) using Metrohm polishing kit, and rinsed with deionized water, followed by sonication in ethanol and deionized water. Finally, the electrode was allowed to dry at room temperature. The modification of the electrodes is as follows: (complete list is provided in Table 1).

Electrode 1: GCE/PPy: The polished GCE was modified with a polypyrrole film (PPy) using pyrrole monomer (0.15 mM). The electropolymerization was done by scanning at the potential −0.6 V to +0.8 V vs Ag/AgCl, at scan rate 100 mV/s to form GCE/PPy.
Electrode 2: GCE/SNGQDs - adsorption of SNGQDs.
The polished GCE was modified with 10 μL of SNGQDs solution (1 mg/mL) in water. The electrode was left to dry for 30 min in an oven at 70 °C.
Electrode 3: GCE/CoPc - adsorption of CoPc.

Similarly, the GCE/CoPc electrode was fabricated by modifying the freshly polished GCE with 10 μL of CoPc (1 μg/mL, dimethyl sulfoxide, DMSO). The modified electrode was left to dry in the oven at 70 °C, for 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodes modified by polymerization of pyrrole in the presence of SNGQDs or CoPc, followed by adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: Electrode 4: (GCE/PPy@SNGQDs): To the electrochemical cell, pyrrole monomer solution (0.15 mM, 1 mL) was added together with 100 μL SNGQDs (1 mg/mL), followed by mechanical stirring and degassing with argon. Electropolymerization was then conducted at the potential window −0.6 V to +0.8 V, at scan rate 100 mV/s and n = 10 cycles.

Electrode 5: (GCE/PPy@CoPc): To the electrochemical cell, 100 μL of CoPc (1 μg/mL, DMSO) was added to pyrrole monomer (0.15 mM, 1 mL) solution, followed by stirring. Electropolymerization was then conducted at the potential window −0.6 V to +0.8 V, at scan rate 100 mV/s and n = 10 cycles.

Electrode 6: (GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc): To GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4), an aliquot (10 μL) of the CoPc in DMSO (1 μg/mL) was added and electrode allowed to dry in the oven for 30 min at 60 °C to give GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc electrode (6). The modification is by adsorption of CoPc.

Electrode 7: (GCE/PPy@CoPc/SNGQDs): To GCE/PPy@CoPc (5), 10 μL of SNGQDs (1 mg/mL) was added. The electrode was then left to dry in the oven for 30 min at 60 °C to give GCE/PPy@CoPc/SNGQDs (7). The modification is by adsorption of SNGQDs.
The following electrodes were modified via sequential adsorption on GCE/PPy (1), they are represented with(seq) at the end:

Electrode 8: (GCE/PPy/SNGQDs(seq)): To a freshly prepared GCE/PPy electrode, 10 μL of SNGQDs (1 mg/mL) was added. The electrode was left to dry in the oven for 30 min, 60 °C to give GCE/PPy/SNGQDs(seq), 8.

Electrode 9: (GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq)): To a freshly prepared GCE/PPy electrode, 10 μL of CoPc (1 μg/mL in DMSO) was added. The electrode was left to dry in the oven for 30 min, 60 °C to give GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq), 9.

Electrode 10: (GCE/PPy/SNGQDs/CoPc(seq)): to GCE/PPy/SNGQDs(seq), 8), 10 μL of CoPc (1 μg/mL), was added. The electrode was left to dry in the oven for 30 min, 60 °C to achieve GCE/PPy/SNGQDs/CoPc(seq), 10.

Electrode 11: (GCE/PPy/CoPc/SNGQDs(seq)): to GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq), 9) an aliquot of the SNGQDs (1 mg/mL in PBS) was added. The electrode was left to dry in the oven for 30 min, 60 °C to achieve to achieve GCE/PPy/CoPc/SNGQD(seq), 11.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aptamer immobilization on to the modified glassy carbon electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: First, different aptamer concentrations (0.1–1.25 μM) were immobilized on the modified electrodes, at room temperature for 30 min for optimization purposes. The optimization was performed on the GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4) surface, and the optimum concentration of 1 μM was obtained (discussed in Section 3.3).

The aptamer was immobilized onto the 10 individual electrodes surface through a covalent amide linkage via NHS/EDC coupling, owing to the carboxylic group rich SNGQDs and CoPc interacting with the amine modified HB5 aptamer.

The following freshly modified electrodes: GCE/SNGQDs (2), GCE/CoPc (3) GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4), GCE/PPy@CoPc (5), GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc (6), GCE/PPy@CoPc/SNGQDs (7), GCE/PPy/SNGQDs(seq) (8), GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq) (9), GCE/PPy/SNGQDs/CoPc(seq), (10) and GCE/PPy/CoPc/SNGQD(seq), (11) were all immersed in a solution of EDC (0.0116 mmol, 0.0018 g) and NHS (0.0304 mmol, 0.0034 g) respectively, for 30 min. Following this, the NHS/EDC activated electrodes were immersed in a HB5 aptamer solution (at the optimum concentration of 1 μM) and left for 30 min at room temperature. In order to remove the unbound aptamer molecules on the electrode surface, the electrodes were washed with PBS (pH 7.4). The electrodes were allowed to air dry. The aptamer modified electrodes (GCE/SNGQDs (2)/HB5, GCE/CoPc (3)/HB5 GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4)/HB5, GCE/PPy@CoPc (5)/HB5, GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc (6)/HB5, GCE/PPy@CoPc/SNGQDs (7)/HB5, GCE/PPy/SNGQDs(seq) (8)/HB5, GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq) (9)/HB5, GCE/PPy/SNGQDs/CoPc(seq), (10)/HB5 and GCE/PPy/CoPc/SNGQD(seq), (11)/HB5), were further treated with 1% BSA to prevent non-specific binding.

Following treatment of all ten electrodes with 1% BSA, they were investigated as probes for the electrochemical detection of HER2. Each electrode was immersed in various HER2 concentrations (1 ng/mL – 10 ng/mL) in PBS for 30 min, a total of eighty electrodes was studied. The electrodes were then rinsed with PBS to remove any non-bonded HER2 off the electrode. Finally, the response of the electrodes upon interaction with HER2 were recorded using the EIS technique.

The selectivity of the designed probes towards the HER2 biomarker was tested using a human serum sample. The male human serum albumin was stored at −20 °C. Serum samples (diluted to 1:500 in PBS) were spiked with HER2 at different concentrations and analysed without further treatments.

The size distribution and shape of the SNGQDs were determined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). A typical TEM image (Fig. S1) shows spherical graphene quantum dots with an average size of 2.8 nm. The X-ray diffraction pattern of the SNGQDs was investigated: a typical XRD pattern for carbon based nanomaterials was observed, with the 002 peak at around 2θ ~ 21°, Fig. S1. The absorption spectra of the cobalt phthalocyanine and SNGQDs used in this work are shown in Fig. S1. The SNGQDs showed an absorption band in the UV region, at 340 nm. The absorption spectrum of the CoPc, showed the characteristic Q-band and Soret band at 661 nm and 342 nm, respectively.

First, the glassy carbon electrode was modified with polypyrrole film by electrochemical deposition of a non-functionalized pyrrole monomer, GCE/PPy (1). The solution of pyrrole monomer (0.15 mM) was scanned from −0.6 V to 0.8 V vs Ag/AgCl, at a scan rate of 100 mV/s in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4). Pyrrole polymerization is evidenced by the increase in current with scan number as reported in literature [27]. The observations in Fig. 1A are consistent with deposition of additional layers of polypyrrole on the layer formed by the first scan, resulting in steadily increasing currents [27]. Secondly, the electrodes GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4) and GCE/PPy@CoPc (5) were constructed. This was performed by electropolymerization of pyrrole onto the GCEs in a mixed solution of 0.15 mM pyrrole with SNGQDs (1 mg/mL) in 0.1 PBS (pH, 7.4) or CoPc (1 mg/mL in DMSO), and scanning through −0.6 V to 0.8 V, 10 cycles at 100 mV/s. Fig. 1B, shows the cyclic voltammetry changes, when pyrrole was polymerized in the presence of CoPc. Cyclic voltammograms similar to those observed during the polymerization of pyrrole alone were observed, hence confirming the formation of PPy in the presence of CoPc, Fig. 1B. The same was observed for polymerization in the presence of the SNGQDs, (Fig. S2).

On optimization, there was an increase in current, decrease in Rct for the PPY with increase in the concentration of monomer pyrrole employed, Fig. S3a,b (Supporting Information). The plots were stopped at 0.15 mM, since there was enough current. Four different concentration of the SNGQDs was explored to investigate the optimum concentration (used for electropolymerization), for the PPy/SNGQDs composite. The CV and EIS data obtained is shown in Fig. S3 c, d, where current decreased with increase in SNGQDs concentration. The highest current was obtained for the lowest concentration of 1 mg/mL, this maybe because GQDs are prone to aggregation at higher concentration. In this study the chosen lowest concentration was 1 mg/mL for SNGQDs and was used throughout. A low concentration of the CoPc (0.1 μg/mL) was used.

In this work, a total of 11 modified electrodes: GCE/PPY (1), GCE/CoPc (2), GCE/SNGQDs (3), GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4), GCE/PPy@CoPc (5), GCE/PPY@SNGQDs/CoPc (6), GCE/PPy@CoPc/SNGQDs (7), GCE/PPy/SNGQDs(seq) (8), GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq) (9), GCE/PPy/SNGQDs/CoPc(seq) (10), and GCE/PPy/CoPc/SNGQDs(seq) (11) were studied. Fig. 2A shows the cyclic voltammograms obtained for a section of electrodes (additional plots are in Fig. S4). The electron transfer of the electrodes was compared in terms of ΔEp for which the values were: bare GCE (68 mV), GCE/SNGQDs (2) (73 mV), GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4) (80 mV), GCE/PPy (1) (154 mV), GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc (6) (166 mV), ~GCE/CoPc (3) (165 mV), GCE/PPy/CoPc/SNGQDs(seq) (11) (246 mV), GCE/PPy/SNGQDs(seq) (8) (288 mV), GCE/PPy@CoPc (5) (361 mV), GCE/PPy@CoPc/SNGQDs (7) (428 mV), GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq) (9) (467 mV), GCE/PPy/SNGQDs/CoPc(seq) (10) (515 mV). The GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4) (80 mV) showed better electron transfer properties compared to the GCE/PPy (1) (154 mV) surface. This might be attributed to SNGQDs containing functional groups (sp2 carbon, S and N atoms, carboxylic, hydroxyl groups) which promote electron transfer between electrolyte and electrode. Also, the structure of the SNGQDs encourage electron transfer via π-π interaction with the pyrrole structure, hence the improved conductivity. The polymerization of PPy in the presence of CoPc and SNGQDs (GCE/PPy@CoPc (5) and GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4), respectively) gave better electron transfer properties compared to the sequentially modified GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq) (9) and GCE/PPy/SNGQDs(seq) (8) counterparts. This may be due to the arrangement and interaction of the nanomaterials on the electrode surface upon modification. During the fabrication of the GCE/PPy@CoPc (5) and GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4) surface, the nanomaterials are mixed in solution and a potential is applied on the electrode. The interaction of the components is governed by each of their structural properties, which then determines how the nanomaterials are arranged on the electrode surface. The applied potential aids for π-π interaction between the pyrrole, SNGQDs and CoPc, and all this takes place on the electrode [28]. Whereas, for the GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq) (9) and GCE/PPy/SNGQDs(seq), 8, the nanomaterials are layered on top of each other. Their interaction and arrangement on the electrode surface is uncontrolled (no applied or external energy) and may sometimes result in passivation of the electrode surface, hence the decreased electron transfer properties. The same trend was observed for the GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc (6) (166 mV) and GCE/PPy/SNGQDs/CoPc(seq) (10) (515 mV) surface, where the sequential adsorption modified surface had poor electron transfer properties compared to polymerization. For the GCE/PPy@CoPc/SNGQDs (7) (428 mV) and GCE/PPy/CoPc/SNGQDs(seq) (11) (246 mV), the surface by sequential adsorption had better electron transfer properties, Table 1. The electrodes containing HB5 and HB5 + HER2 showed an increase in ΔEp, hence a decrease in conductivity, Table 1 (electrode 4 as an example) and Fig. 2.

EIS is well known as an effective tool for studying the interface properties of surface modified electrodes [29,30]. A typical impedance spectrum, presented in the form of the Nyquist plot, includes a semicircle portion at higher frequencies corresponding to the electron transfer limited process and a linear part at lower frequency range representing diffusion limited process. EIS measurement was carried out for the various electrodes, Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. 2B (more plots in Fig. S4). The data obtained were fitted using an equivalent modified circuit, Fig. 3 . The key parameter used to differentiate and characterize the modified electrodes was the charge transfer resistance (Rct), which increases with decrease in conductivity and vice versa. The Rct value (Table 1) is the highest for 10 corresponding as was the case for ΔEp value for this electrode. Otherwise there is no clear trend between Rct and ΔEp.

First the concentration of aptamer was optimized. There were six different aptamer concentrations explored: 0.1 μM, 0.25 μM, 0.5 μM, 0.75 μM, 1.0 μM and 1.25 μM using GCE/PPy@SNGQDs. An increase in Rct with an increase in aptamer concentration was observed, Fig. S5. This trend was observed until at concentration 1.0 μM, and it did not increase significantly 1.25 μM. Therefore, 1.0 μM was regarded as the optimum aptamer concentration. Important to note, prior to use the aptamer was denatured following the manufacturers protocol.

The modified electrodes: GCE/CoPc (2)/HB5, GCE/SNGQDs (3)/HB5, GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4)/HB5, GC/PPy@CoPc (5)/HB5, GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc (6)/HB5, GCE/PPy@CoPc/SNGQDs (7)/HB5, GCE/PPy/SNGQDs(seq) (8)/HB5, GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq) (9)/HB5, GCE/PPy/SNGQDs/CoPc(seq) (10)/HB5, and GCE/PPy/CoPc/SNGQDs(seq) (11)/HB5 were tested towards the electrochemical detection of HER2. The various electrodes were all incubated in PBS solution containing varying amounts of HER2 (0 to 10 ng/mL). Fig. 4 shows EIS Nyquist plots for the electrodes GCE/PPy@CoPc/SNGQDs (7)/HB5 and GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq) (9)/HB5 as examples, obtained in 10 mM PBS buffer of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KCl and 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4-, additional data is presented in Fig. S6 and Table S1 (supporting Information). An increase in the Rct with an increase in the HER2 concentration for all electrodes was observed. We have compared the electrodes as pyrrole polymerized with CoPc or SNGQs and sequential as follows: GCE/PPy@CoPc (5)/HB5 vs. GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq) (9)/HB5, GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4)/HB5 vs. GCE/PPy/SNGQDs(seq) (8)/HB5, GCE/PPy@CoPc/SNGQDs (7)/HB5 vs. GCE/PPy/CoPc/SNGQDs(seq) (11)/HB5 detection of HER2, Table S1. For these electrodes Rct values were higher for sequential modifications at all HER2 concentrations except for 4 and 8 at 9 ng/mL. For GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc (6)/HB5 vs. GCE/PPy/SNGQDs/CoPc(seq) (10)/HB5 the sequential modification (10) gave lower values than 6. With aptamer-modified electrode, Rct increases again because more hindering of access of the redox couple to the electrode surface resulting in low electron transfer efficiency, Table 1. This might be attributed to the structural flexibility of the aptamer as an attribute, where the folding and unfolding conformation variation affects the electron transfer properties of the modified surface [31].

There are certain static and dynamic attributes that every biosensor possesses, from which the biosensor performance is reflected; sensitivity is one of them. Typically, a biosensor is required to detect analyte concentration of as low as ng/ml or even fg/ml to confirm the presence of traces of analytes in a sample. The slope (m) of the regression plots in Fig. 4 (and Fig. S6) is the sensitivity (the values listed in Table 2 ) of the biosensor. A higher sensitivity value was obtained for the GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc (6)/HB5 (71,584 Ωng−1mL) which is the probe formed by polymerizing pyrrole in the presence of SNGQDs followed by adsorption of CoPc. The sensitivity of a biosensor depends on various factors, and particularly for this work we observed the role of surface functionalization. The different modification techniques play an important role in how the HB5 aptamer is immobilized on the electrode surface and in turn its interaction with HER2. It has been specified in the method section, that the HB5 aptamer is immobilized via a covalent amide linkage on the electrode surface. This immobilization is dependent on the –COOH groups on the nanomaterials on the electrode surface, which vary in exposure or availability depending on the electrode modification technique. The sequentially modified probe GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq) (9)/HB5 had a higher sensitivity (28,741 Ωng−1mL) compared to the pyrrole polymerized GCE/PPy@CoPc (5)/HB5 probe, 4285 Ωng−1mL, Table 2. While both electrodes contain the CoPc and PPy, the arrangement of the nanomaterials differ. The CoPc on the GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq) (9)/HB5 is layered on the PPy modified surface; the carboxylic groups and the redox cobalt metal are exposed. This surface modification holds as both an immobilization and signal amplification platform. The GCE/PPy@CoPc (5)/HB5 probe is made from the mixing of both the PPy and CoPc on the electrode surface. The interaction is unarranged; aggregation is a possibility as well as shielding of the Co metal. The CoPc can possibly be wrapped within the polymer and not as exposed, where the surface serves mostly as an immobilization platform and little signal amplification. Similarly, for the GCE/PPy/SNGQDs (seq) (8)/HB5 and GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4)/HB5 probes. The former showed better sensitivity (53,006 Ωng−1mL) compared to the latter (11,457 Ωng−1mL), Table 2. It is obvious that the SNGQDs containing probes showed better sensitivity towards HER2 compared to the CoPc containing probes regardless of the modification technique. This might be due to the fact that, the average size of a phthalocyanine is ~1 nm [32], whilst that of SNGQDs is ~2.8 nm (determined by TEM in this work, S1). Compared to the CoPc, the large particle size of the SNGQDs allows for more HB5 particles as well as conformational flexibility of the HB5 aptamer. When all nanomaterials (PPy, SNGQDs and CoPc) are combined on the electrode surface, the probes modified by polymerization of pyrrole in the presence of CoPc or SNGQDs (electrodes 6 and 7), had better sensitivity values compared to those by sequential adsorption (electrodes 10 and 11, respectively). This might be due to aggregation and passivation during the sequential loading of the nanomaterials, where little signal amplification exists and immobilization platform support dominates. Whereas, for the electrodes modified by polymerization of pyrrole in the presence of CoPc or SNGQDs the π-π interaction of either PPy and CoPc or PPy and SNGQDs are governed by the applied potential, which almost affords the nanomaterials an orderly arrangement on the electrode surface. The opposite was observed, when only two components are present (electrodes 4 and 5 compared to 8 and 9, respectively). This might be attributed the interaction and arrangement of the CoPc and pyrrole on the GCE, assuming that during polymerization in the presence of the CoPc, the phthalocyanine is imbedded onto the polymer and hidden. Thus, there is more exposure of the CoPc and its carboxylic groups in the sequential modification allowing for more sites for interaction with the HB5 aptamer. The lowest sensitivity value was obtained for GCE/CoPc (2)/HB5. The combination of the SNGQDs with polypyrrole (GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4)/HB5 or GCE/PPy/SNGQDs (8)/HB5) improved the sensitivity of the GCE/SNGQDs (3)/HB5. Similarly, the sensitivity of the CoPc, was improved upon combination with polypyrrole: GCE/PPy@CoPc (5)/HB5 or GCE/PPy/CoPc (9)/HB5. Reports have shown, that the combination of various polymers with different nanostructures result in improved mechanical, thermal and electrical properties [33]. As such, our results demonstrate the improvement of the electrochemical detection of the HER2 antigen, when polypyrrole is combined with SNGQDs and/or CoPc on a glassy carbon electrode. This speaks mainly to the structural compatibility of the nanomaterials (π-π interactions), where their interaction upon combination results in better electrochemical sensors compared to the individual nanoamaterials. This shows the role of combining various nanomaterials to improving sensor characteristics, and as such the motivation behind this work towards the design of novel HER2 sensors.

The limit of detection (LOD) values for all probes were estimated by the equation 3σ/s. In which σ is the standard deviation of Ret values obtained for PBS (without HER2) respectively for all probes, and s is the sensitivity (the slope) of the regression plots. The best LOD value was for GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc (6)/HB5 at 0.00141 ng/mL and the highest LOD value was for the GCE/CoPc (2)/HB5, 0.647 ng/mL, Tables 2 and 3 . Electrodes where pyrrole is polymerized in the presence of CoPc or NGQDs followed by their addition on top (GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc (6) and GCE/PPy@CoPc/SNGQDs (7) gave the lowest LoD, with electrode 6 being the best. The reason for the low detection limit for GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc (6) could be a result of the CoPc on top with enhanced electron transfer better since CoPc derivatives are known to improve electron transfer. The LOD values reported in this work were found to be comparable (and sometimes better) to the ones reported in literature on aptamer based detection of HER2 [34–43], Table 3. This makes the probes reported in this work to be possible competitors in HER2 biosensor design. There are reports of better performing electrochemical sensors towards the HER2 detection [42,43]. However, the current immunosensor possesses some advantages which make it superior to the previously reported works. These include simplicity, cost effectiveness and the detection of HER2 levels in human serum samples.

The reproducibility of the designed probes was investigated by running successive measurements at each HER2 concentrations in triplicate and the standard deviation was calculated. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for all electrodes was for all ten electrodes at various concentrations was determined, Table S1. The electrodes in this work had % RSD (n = 3, at each HER2 concentration) acceptable for biosensors, less than 8%. This held for all probes except for the electrodes GCE/PPy@CoPc (5)/HB5, GCE/PPy@CoPc/SNGQDs (7)/HB5, GCE/PPy/CoPc (seq) (9)/HB5 and GCE/PPy/SNGQDs/CoPc (seq) (10)/HB5. This again might be attributed to the spatial arrangement of the nanomaterials on the electrode, which affects the way in which the HB5 is attached on the surface and therefore exposed to the interaction of HER2 upon detection.

After a 4 days of storage, the aptasensors were used to detect HER2 which allowed for the determination of their stability. By looking at the difference between Rct values, the electrodes maintained 90% of their performance ability, proving to be quite stable in that time frame.

.Finally, the feasibility of the designed aptasensors for real life analysis of HER2 was investigated. Five known concentrations of HER2 were spiked in normal human serum diluted 500-fold in PBS and measured, Table 4 and Table S2 (Supporting Information). The specificity of the various probes was investigated in terms of percentage recoveries (at the highest concentration of 9 ng/mL), and was shown to increase in the order: GCE/PPy/CoPc/SNGQDs(seq) (11)/HB5 > GCE/PPy/SNGQDs/CoPc(seq) (10)/HB5 > GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4)/HB5 > GCE/SNGQDs (2)/HB5 > GCE/PPy@CoPc/SNGQDs (7)/HB5 > GCE/PPy/CoPc(seq) (9)/HB5 > GCE/CoPc (3)/HB5 > GCE/PPy/SNGQDs(seq) (8)/HB5 ≅ GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc (6)/HB5 > GCE/PPy@CoPc (5)/HB5. Thus sequential addition gave higher recoveries when both CoPc and SNGQDs are present. CoPc alone with PPy, performed the worst showing the importance of SNGQDs in the design of the probes in this work. Overall the probe GCE/PPy@SNGQDs (4)/HB5 compared to the rest of the probes, had % recoveries higher than 70% at all concentrations (1 ng/mL, 3 ng/mL, 5 ng/mL, 7 ng/mL and 9 ng/mL). The sensor is sensitive to HER2 protein and has considerable potential as an effective alternative method for the early detection of HER2 in human serum. This result shows the promise of the application of the probe in real life application for HER2 detection. The probes all had a percentage recovery higher than 100% at 1 ng/mL HER2.

In the present work, the contribution of the sensor interface on the detection of HER2was investigated. The role of combining two or more nanomaterials on the electrode surface in sensor design was investigated. Two different modification techniques of the GCE using graphene quantum dots, a phthalocyanine and polypyrrole were explored. The modification techniques included (i) GCE is modified via polymerization of pyrrole in the presence of either SNGQDs (PPy@SNGQDs) or CoPc (PPy@CoPc), and (ii) GCE is modified via sequential adsorption of nanomaterials following pyrrole electropolymerization (e.g. PPy/SNGQDs(seq) or PPy/CoPc(seq)). In general, the electrodes modified by sequential adsorption had better electron transfer properties (ΔEp, Rct) compared to their counter PPy/nanomaterial polymerization modified electrodes. The modified electrodes were used as immobilization platforms for the HB5 aptamer. The results obtained showed the role of combining various nanomaterials on the electrode surface for improved sensory characteristics. The electrodes with single nanomaterials GCE/CoPc and GCE/SNGQDs were the least performing towards the detection of HER2 compared to the rest of the electrodes. The best performing electrode towards the electrochemical detection of HER2 was GCE/PPy@SNGQDs/CoPc (6) with a limit of detection value 1.41 pm/mL. The results showed that the method is simple and sensitive enough for the determination of HER2 in serum samples with good reproducibility and accuracy. The probes herein, have considerable potential for application in the early detection of HER2 as a breast cancer biomarker.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), and Rhodes University.

Acta 186 (2019) 495. doi:10.1007/s00604-019-3619-y

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive sandwich-type voltammetric immunosensor for breast cancer biomarker HER2 detection based on gold nanoparticles decorated cu-MOF and Cu2ZnSnS4 NPs/Pt/g-C3N4 composite

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Yola, Sensitive sandwich-type voltammetric immunosensor for breast cancer biomarker HER2 detection based on gold nanoparticles decorated Cu-MOF and Cu2ZnSnS4 NPs/Pt/g-C3N4 composite, Michrochim. Acta (2021) 78. doi:10.1007/s00604-021-04735-y
